one_paragraph_summary: Delivered April 27, 2026 at the Main Lounge of the Manila Polo Club, 40 days after Linda Manuel Mañalac's passing on March 21, 2026, this eulogy by CJP — himself a three-year widower following Leni Panganiban's sudden death on April 9, 2023 — opens by thanking Fr. Francis Alvarez SJ for the yoke parable (Christ as the strong ox bearing more of the burden) and frames the address through the 'fragility of life' theme: Linda was in and out of Makati Med ICU for three months, pronounced healed and ready for discharge, then died unexpectedly. CJP builds a cumulative named-witness canon — Linda's son Carlos 'Pop' Mañalac on her 'complete, absolute, unconditional generosity'; common granddaughter Andrea on her presence at every important moment; nephew Rico Manuel (Ayala Land) on Ninang Linda as 'the glue that held our family together'; Sonya Garcia on the day Linda paid the full price for the undeveloped Alfonso, Cavite estate (that became Sonya's Garden) without inspecting it; Sister Cho Borromeo on 30 years of Linda hosting the Franciscan Missionaries of Mary (25 in Pasay + 5 in Forbes Park); Fr. Robert Manansala on Linda's love for God, family, the Southeastern College community, and the poor; and even Tagaytay-road street vendors of boiled corn and camote. He distills this as 'Generosity Personified' and, in his own coinage, 'Love and Magnanimity Magnified.' His personal reminiscences include the pearl-necklace gift Linda gave at her own birthday party to a first-time-met retired SC justice ('There, bagay na bagay sa iyo'), Punta Fuego fishermen waiting because she paid above asking price, and the 'Happy Foursome' / 'Sousai 100 Travel and Gourmet Barkada' travels of Tong & Linda Manalac with Tony & Leni Panganiban through Morocco, the Danube, San Francisco, Rome, Madrid, Paris, Saigon, and Hong Kong. He frames Linda's spirituality as Ignatian deed-over-word — 'giving without counting the cost' — and closes with an explicit public promise to fellow-widower Tong ('walking and breathing the fresh morning air at the Manila Polo Oval... Hacienda Linda... beaches of Batangas... gold tees of Manila Golf... shell courts of Manila Polo'), generalized as a pastoral exhortation to all widows and widowers to 'look back on the past with gratitude and quiet surrender' and to live fully until 'a joyful reunion with them, together with all His angels, in His Heavenly Kingdom.'

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered August 29, 2025 at Manila Polo Club as closing remarks of the annual FLP Awards Ceremony, this speech opens with extended, witty acknowledgments of the head-table guests led by Special Guest First Lady Louise Araneta-Marcos (whose birthday on August 21 was celebrated by the audience and whose 'Germany secret' exchange with CJP at the August 1 Goldenberg Mansion First Friday Musical is teased but not revealed), guest speaker Fernando Zobel de Ayala (subjected to a teasing roster of admiring lady guests), Lucio 'Hun-Hun' Tan III ('young, single and available'), Mike Toledo (defused with a St. Peter henpecked-husband joke), SAJ Telly Bernabe, bachelor donor Dr. Allen Roxas (who gave FLP a 2,750 sq m commercial lot in Alabang Global City), MPIC boss Chaye Cabal Revilla, Gov. 'Say' Tetangco, SolGen Lelen Berberabe, J. Jhosep 'Jholo' Lopez, and CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo (who will lay the FLP Museum's cornerstone before his November 2026 retirement). CJP then reports on FLP's five education programs and announces a new Super Committee — chaired by SolGen Berberabe with SAJ Bernabe, Bernadine Siy (first female Ateneo BOT chair), Atty. Joel Gregorio, Atty. Arvin Cortez, and Gov. Tetangco as adviser — to design a program elevating lawyers' ethical standards, prompted by Tessie Sy Coson's principle that lawyers must be 'not just talented, but also ethical and Godly.' He then presents a 7-minute video on the two flagship ultimate projects: the futuristic AI-powered Museum for Liberty and Prosperity (designed in preliminary form by Architect Jun Palafox) and the pro-poor, multi-billion-peso Fund for Liberty and Prosperity for MSMEs. Invoking 'to whom much is given, much is required' and a seed-in-fertile-soil parable, he closes with the hope of gathering 'one day within my lifetime' at the new Museum, and turns the program over to the FLP Choir for 'Tagumpay Nating Lahat' arranged by Eudenice Palaruan.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered as closing remarks at the FLP Awards Ceremony at Manila Polo Club on September 2, 2024, this speech has two parts. The first thanks the special guests seated around Panganiban's table, organized counterclockwise — Special Guest First Lady Louise Araneta-Marcos (whose birthday is being doubly celebrated since the ceremony was rescheduled from August 19 to a date sacred to her), guest speaker MVP (Manuel V. Pangilinan), CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo (chair of the Law Scholarship Board of Judges), Justice Ramon Paul L. Hernando, Lucio 'Hun Hun' Tan III (Tan Yan Kee Foundation), Solomon Hermosura (representing Ayala Corporation), Arthur and Alfred Ty (Metrobank Foundation), former BSP Governor 'Say' Tetangco (SM Investments), and FLP President Justice Angelina Sandoval Gutierrez. The second part lays out Panganiban's foundational thesis: all five Philippine Constitutions are libertarian in their Bills of Rights, but political libertarianism of the mind must be paired with liberation from extreme poverty — freedom for the spirit AND food for the body, freedom from fear AND freedom from want, under the rule of law, not dictators. He inventories FLP's merit-based liberty programs (Professorial Chairs, Law Scholarships, Dissertation Writing Contests) and prosperity programs (ESMEL Fellowships, scaling from 5 to 10) plus the Panganiban Educational Assistance Program (need-based). He announces FLP's two 'ultimate projects' for the 2021-2031 second decade — the Center for Liberty and Prosperity and the Prosperity Fund — seeded by his family's 357-square-meter Paseo de Magallanes lot (≥ ₱100M valuation, FLP-titled since 2022), with additional pledges including a 2,500 sq m southern-suburb commercial lot, and closes with a fervent call for cooperation.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered January 16, 2024 at the National Museum of Fine Arts during the 1971-72 Constitutional Convention Surviving Delegates' Conference and Exhibits on 'Philippine Constitutions and the Social Order,' this speech opens with an admission against interest ('I do not feel qualified... I AM NOT A ROBOT') and a joke about three new commandments added by scheming lawyers and unfaithful husbands ('Thou shall not get caught'; 'deny, deny, deny, even when caught in flagrante'; invoke the right to remain silent). Borrowing the judicial opinion structure (FACTS — ISSUE — RULING), CJP situates the 1987 Constitution — ratified February 2, 1987 and in force February 11, 1987 — within the global 'AI Winter' of 1973-1993, demonstrating with the ILOVEYOU virus case (May 2000, $5B+ damages, two Filipino programmers acquitted because PH had no anti-malware law) that constitutional anticipation of technology was structurally impossible. He then enumerates seven contemporary pressure points: (1) in vitro fertilization and surrogacy (citing Pope Francis's January 8, 2024 ambassadors speech calling surrogacy 'despicable'); (2) nanotechnology's dual nature; (3) autonomous weapons that select targets without human intervention; (4) cyber risks and AI errors in the hands of terrorists, dictators, and warlords; (5) social-media-driven youth suicide (more US young people die by suicide than by soldiers/terrorists/criminals combined); (6) the illusory truth effect in political ads ('Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas' with 1M+ X views); and (7) the 100% Filipino-mass-media ownership constraint in an era of CNN/BBC and foreign social platforms. His ruling: humane justice can be dispensed ONLY by judges, not by automated formula — the Penal Code's range of penalties and the Civil Code's 'right and justice' liberality clause confer discretion irreducible by tech bots. He cites the UK House of Lords March 30, 2022 'Technology Rules?' report ('a new Wild West'), the November 2023 Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit (29 governments including the Philippines), and US CJ Roberts's 2023 Year-End Report confirming judges are not becoming obsolete. He endorses CJ Gesmundo's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovations 2022-2027 — 'turn tech for good and move from problem to solution' — as the right institutional posture, and closes by invoking the 1987 Constitution's preamble guarantee of human dignity 'especially the poor, the oppressed, the dispossessed, the weak, the disabled and the marginalized' as the irreducible reference point for all technology policy.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered November 9, 2023 at the Makati Shangri-La as extemporaneous closing remarks of the MVP Group's Annual Corporate Governance Enhancement Session, this short address showcases CJP's witty register at a corporate-governance audience. Opening with a self-deprecating mock-rank-comparison — he is not the great MVP (Manuel V. Pangilinan) but a 'lowly AVP' (Artemio V. Panganiban), a 'fake' — he punishes the audience with 'I'll prolong my talk so you won't eat,' then performs an object-anecdote with a borrowed Grand Hyatt ballpen used to take notes at the Makati Shangri-La event, borrowed from the Grand Hyatt's owner Alfred Ty seated beside him. He thanks guest lecturer Dr. Max Tegmark and panel reactor Paco Sandejas (disclosing the family tie: Paco is the brother of CJP's deceased son-in-law Thomas Sandejas; Paco and CJP's son both UP summas and Stanford PhDs). He then delivers three takeaways: (1) invest in human intelligence as contradistinguished from AI — 'artificial intelligence is fake, human intelligence is real. So, we will invest in all of you, in all your brains. We will just make AI our slave'; (2) customer-centricity as North Star — 'we will use artificial intelligence, native intelligence, Filipino intelligence, PLDT intelligence to serve our customers'; and (3) leverage the MVP Group's collective strength across PLDT, Meralco, Maynilad, MPH, and tollways. He closes by posing the governance questions the executives must answer — customer data protection, transactional integrity, cybersecurity, and respectful competition — and asks: 'Are we up to the challenge?'

Gesmundo and seven other Supreme Court justices (a majority of the 15-member Court), and announcing his handover of ALA Philippines chairmanship to CJ Gesmundo effective January 1, 2024; (2) thanking Malaysian host CJ Tun Tengku Maimun binti Tuan Mat and acknowledging ALA President CJ Sundaresh Menon for honoring his late wife Leni (d. April 9, 2023) with a minute of silence at the Opening Ceremony; (3) declaring ASEAN's 40-year history of consensus and rule of law as the only sustainable method of regional governance — explicitly rejecting 'gray tactics,' military force, mob rule, propaganda, and nuclear weapons, and affirming faith in the UN, ICJ, WTO Appellate Body, and the Permanent Court of Arbitration; and (4) reporting FLP's scoreboard (83 law scholarships, 16 professorial chairs, 27 dissertation winners, 10 ESMEL fellowships) and announcing FLP's two ultimate projects — the Center for Liberty and Prosperity with an AI-powered museum, and a multi-billion-peso Prosperity Fund — which he hopes to complete 'before I leave this earth.'

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered August 11, 2022 as online closing remarks at FLP's Awards Ceremony — shifted from face-to-face at AIM to virtual format because of the Omicron surge — this speech frames the foundation's decade-transition thesis: the first decade (2011-2021) was concentrated on LIBERTY programs (Professorial Chairs with Metrobank, Scholarship with Tan Yan Kee Foundation, Dissertation Writing with Ayala); the second decade (2021-2031) will broaden into PROSPERITY programs starting with the ESMEL Fellowships at ₱450,000 each (in partnership with MPIC and MVP, MOA drafted by CFO Chaye Cabal-Revilla and bar-topper Chief Legal Counsel Jun Pilares III). CJP discloses his MPIC independent directorship as a corporate-governance modeling. He thanks CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo as Scholarship Board of Judges chair and adopts the CJ's keynote formulation — 'safeguarding rights go hand in hand with nurturing prosperity' — as the canonical SC-articulation of FLP's symbiotic philosophy. He thanks J. Ramon Paul L. Hernando (Dissertation Board) and Gov. Amando 'Say' Tetangco Jr. (ESMEL Board). He then announces the AIM Master in International Business Law (MIBL) curriculum, designed over 8 months with President/Dean Jikyeong Kang and faculty Olivier Roche and Felipe Calderon — inspired by his grandson Miguel Panganiban Sandejas's unusual Babson MBA + Northwestern MSL trajectory (graduate law without undergraduate law) — and invites Ateneo, La Salle, UP, and UST to follow AIM's lead. He acknowledges Tan Yan Kee Foundation (six years of scholarship funding, including 21-slot expansion to resolve a tie), Ayala Corporation (5 years of Dissertation Writing), Tony Tan Caktiong + Injap Sia (Hotel 101 condo donation for poor-student assistance), and Justice Angelina Sandoval Gutierrez (Acting FLP President). He closes by announcing FLP's two ultimate projects — the Center for Liberty and Prosperity with interactive immersive Museum (liberty side, with SC partnership) and the ₱1B+ 'Entrepreneurship Fund' for MSMEs (prosperity side, with pro bono viability study by Cezar Consing) — and asks the audience to pray for their attainment.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered July 7, 2022 at the Carmelite Missionaries Center of Spirituality Chapel in Tagaytay, at the Eucharistic Celebration presided by Cardinal Jose F. Advincula marking the Panganiban Diamond Wedding (61st) Anniversary in coincidence with the CBCP Annual Plenary Assembly, this is Panganiban's most extended spiritual autobiography. Thanking four named sponsors (Cardinal Advincula, Archbishop Villegas, Bishop David, Sister Marigold) with ₱50,000 envelopes each, he tells his journey of faith: born in impoverished Mandili, Candaba; childhood in Sampaloc selling newspapers and shoeshines; 'unchurched and uncatechized' until weekly Baclaran novenas with his father; pre-Vatican-II Latin Masses he could not follow; 1947 framed Mother of Perpetual Help image that traveled from Sampaloc to Canada and back over 75 years; FEU law school under Fr. Michael Nolan and Dean Jovito Salonga; courting Leni Carpio (NUS counterpart from St. Scholastica's) whose 'magical Yes' required monthly confession and Sunday Mass at Cubao Immaculate Conception; St. Scholastica Sisters and Fr. Anselmo Bustos disapproving because Leni was novitiate-bound; wedding on April 8, 1961; 1986 Marriage Encounter (cerebral) and Life in the Spirit Seminar (LSS — 'soul-piercing,' vision of risen Christ, 'spiritual tongues of fire'); catechism with Monsignor Gerry Santos; reading 100+ Christian books including Barclay's 16-volume Daily Study Bible; Ninang Tita de Villa drawing him into the Council of the Laity, PCP-II (1991), PPCRV, the Pontifical Council for the Laity in the Vatican (1996-2002 — the only Filipino + only incumbent SC justice so appointed), and the Manila Cathedral retrofitting foundation. Ramos offered him Justice Secretary in June 1992; CBCP was split (Archbishop Legaspi urging decline, Bishop Bacani urging accept); Leni broke the tie with her veto on politicians' wives; Ramos responded 'Under the saya ka pala... Dalawa na tayo' and redirected him to the SC. The most quoted anecdote: in January 2006 he told then-Sandiganbayan-nominee Alex Gesmundo to 'sit on my chair because one day you will be sitting there anyway as chief justice' — a prophecy fulfilled and recounted with Gesmundo present at the anniversary.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered February 6, 2021 as edited extemporaneous closing remarks at the online Awards Ceremony for the Fourth Year FLP Law Scholars (AY 2020-2021), this speech is structurally the most personally disclosing of CJP's FLP addresses. He thanks the FLP Board of Trustees present (Edilberto de Jesus, Justice Angelina Sandoval-Gutierrez, his wife Prof. Leni Panganiban, daughter Marilen Yaptangco, Treasurer Becky Felix, Executive Director Susan Gavino as 'mother hen'), the Tan Yan Kee Foundation (represented by Valerie Ann L. Tan, niece of Dr. Lucio Tan) for six years of unbroken Scholarship funding through COVID, and congratulates the nine fourth-year graduating scholars whose checks will be deposited the following Monday. He explains the 20-scholars-per-year program structure (10 third-year + 10 fourth-year), the Preliminary Selection Committee under Atty. Joel Gregorio, and the final-interview Board of Judges chaired by the incumbent Chief Justice or a Senior Associate Justice — 'in the inner sanctum of the Court.' The speech's emotional center is a vulnerable personal disclosure: none of his five children and none of his ten grandchildren entered law, leaving him frustrated that his legacy 'would come to an end' — he self-deprecatingly mentions briefly considering 'adopting young people who might become lawyers or even... donating to – what do you call that? – sperm banks' before settling on FLP as the better legacy vehicle. Forty scholars across four batches now constitute that legacy. He validates the program by reciting bar topnotchers — Batch 1's Sean Borja (No. 1) and Katrina Gaw (No. 5), Batch 2's Kenneth Manuel (No. 6) and Dexter Rojas (No. 9) — and previews the FLP 10th Anniversary projects (Coffee Table Book edited by Joel Gregorio with FLPSS support, funded by a ₱3M Metrobank Foundation grant, plus a separate Professorial Lectures volume) launching before October 10, 2021. He describes himself, at 84, as 'still trying to operate my laptop, my iPad, my desktop, of course, my smart cell phone.'

notable_anecdotes: ["Lambino vs Comelec (October 25, 2006): At 8:00 AM on voting day, an unnamed justice visited Panganiban's chambers to defect — a former President to whom he owed his career had asked him to vote with the seven led by Justice Puno. Panganiban hurriedly summoned Carpio and asked him to prepare a dissent for the expected defeat. Carpio offered to resign in protest; Panganiban said he should be the one to resign since he had only two months left and Carpio had thirteen years; Carpio: 'Let us both resign and show that we can sacrifice our dearest treasures for what we believe in.' one_paragraph_summary: Delivered on October 18, 2019 at New World Hotel Makati at a tribute dinner sponsored by the Makati Business Club, MAP, and FINEX, this is Panganiban's eyewitness biography of Antonio T. Carpio's five missed chances at the Chief Justiceship across his 18-year Supreme Court career. The first chance (May 2010, post-Puno) was declined for 'delicadeza' — Carpio had voted against the midnight-appointment decision authorizing the very vacancy, refusing to be 'Mr. Midnight Chief Justice'; the chiefship went to Corona. The second (May 2012, post-Corona impeachment) was lost when Aquino III told Carpio directly that he admired his qualifications but key Senate allies and three business leaders opposed him. The third (post-Sereno quo warranto) was again declined for delicadeza — Carpio had dissented from the ouster decision; De Castro got the post. The fourth (post-De Castro) was accepted, but Duterte appointed third-most-senior Bersamin over both Carpio and Peralta. The fifth (October 2019, post-Bersamin) was offered as a one- or two-day appointment but declined to avoid being 'Mr. One Day Chief Justice.' The Court unanimously granted Carpio CJ-level salary and retirement benefits. Panganiban frames the 'best news' as still to come: when China accedes to the rule of law over the West Philippine Sea, Carpio's single-minded advocacy will earn him acclamation as 'a hero and a patriot.' Panganiban interleaves Carpio's near-resignation with him over Lambino vs Comelec (2006) and Carpio's last-minute Edsa 2 pleading that made Arroyo regular rather than Acting President. He closes by leading the assembly in three 'Hep, hep! Hurray!' acclamations of Carpio as the people's Chief Justice.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered December 20, 2016 at the brand-new Maybank Performing Arts Theater in BGC at the end of the 'Ageless Passion' musicale staged in his honor, this closing remarks address blends humor, gratitude, and doxology. Panganiban opens by introducing his wife Leni, his five children with their spouses, and his eight grandchildren by name. He greets Cardinal Luis Antonio Tagle ('the gentle Archbishop of Manila and Pope-in-waiting,' who sang rather than recited the Prayer of St. Francis as invocation), President FVR and wife Ming (who sent his book '2030 One World, One Community, One Family' with the salutation 'Dear Tiger Chief Justice Art'), and President GMA and husband Atty. Jose Miguel (who appointed him 21st CJ without interview or conversation from the JBC's 5-name short list). He discloses that December 20 is not his actual birthday (which is December 7) but the 11th anniversary of his CJ appointment, and recalls the hotel-ballroom moment when, mid-speech, someone passed him a note that he had been appointed CJ — he froze, descended the stage, avoided media, drove with Leni to their parish church, and on bended knees thanked the Lord. He then reformulates Reinhold Niebuhr's serenity prayer for old age: 'Lord, grant me the senility to forget the people I never liked, the good fortune to run into the people I do, and the eyesight to tell the difference,' and quotes Frank Laubach's 'Life Begins at 80' on the joys of octogenarian forgetfulness. He inventories his life at 80 — director/adviser in 12+ corporations and 6+ foundations with near-100% attendance, six annual trips abroad, tennis and golf four times a week, gym thrice weekly, occasional dancing — having survived 'premature death as a six-year old during World War II' to become 'a recycled octogenarian.' He closes by reciting the personal Psalm-23-echoing prayer he composed in 1995 on entering the Supreme Court, dedicating his life to God.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered as keynote at the JCI-Manila General Membership Meeting at Tower Club on April 19, 2016, Panganiban opens with a self-deprecating lawyer-joke (three sons asked to put ₱100K each in the dead father's coffin; the lawyer writes a ₱300K check and takes ₱200K cash as change), then frames the RVR Award — co-sponsored by JCI-Manila, AIM, and the Del Rosario family since 2009, with Panganiban as annual Board of Judges chair — as a uniquely Filipino business-culture artifact that insists on the inseparability of entrepreneurship and social responsibility. He names the seven past RVR awardees (Bacani, Tambunting, Oscar M. Lopez, Sycip, Paterno, Zobel de Ayala, Pangilinan) as the canon of business statesmanship. He introduces FLP (founded 2011, five years after retirement and on his 75th birthday) as the institutionalization of his twin-beacons philosophy — justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, peace and development — and argues using a three-country Asian comparison (Deng's China, South Korea's private initiative, North Korea's suppression of entrepreneurship) that the way to conquer poverty is to UNLEASH the entrepreneurial genius of people. He closes by challenging the Jaycees themselves to become, like the RVR awardees, icons of entrepreneurship and nation building.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered November 29, 2012 at Manila Golf Club for ACCRA Law's 40th Anniversary BusinessLaw Forum jointly with the Management Association of the Philippines, Panganiban uses three of his own landmark ponencias to lay out his settled jurisprudential philosophy of 'liberty and prosperity under the rule of law' — a phrase he credits to Canada's CJ Beverley McLachlin after the 2006 Global Forum. His two-track theory: in political/civil-liberty cases the scales weigh heavily against the government via strict scrutiny; in economic/policy cases courts must defer to the Executive and Congress unless there is grave abuse of discretion or blatant constitutional violation. He demonstrates this with (1) Tanada vs Angara (1997), the unanimous decision he authored upholding the Senate's WTO Treaty ratification by holding that the Constitution balances bias-for-Filipino-goods against business reciprocity, and that the 'wisdom or viability' of globalization is outside judicial review; (2) La Bugal-B'laan vs Ramos (Dec 1, 2004 MR 10-4 reversing the Jan 27, 2004 original 8-5 ruling), where he authored the reconsideration upholding the Mining Law, the IRR, and the FTAA on the principle that the Constitution must be read 'in broad, life-giving strokes' rather than to 'strangulate economic growth'; and (3) Gamboa vs Teves (June 28, 2011), where he diplomatically critiques the SC majority for departing from deferential interpretation by reading 'capital' in Art XII Sec 11 to mean only voting common shares — siding implicitly with the dissenters who argued the 1935 framers' intent covered all shares. He notes that 2012 Philippine GDP growth (6.3%/5.9%/7.1% across Q1/Q2/Q3, best in ASEAN, second in Asia after China) shows the country's economic momentum is real and worth protecting — and closes by reiterating that the recognized engine of growth in a free enterprise regime is the private sector, to which the judiciary should defer.

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered May 24, 2007 at the Canossian Academy in Lipa City for the Canossian Sisters' 200th foundation day, this is Panganiban's most compact autobiography. He opens with a medieval-farmer parable — horse runs away (bad news), comes back with ten wild horses (good news), son breaks leg (bad news), king conscripts the village and only the lame son survives (good news) — with the farmer's refrain 'Good news, bad news, who knows? I will just keep on trying my best and God will do the rest' becoming the speech's interpretive scaffolding. He then walks through his real story: post-WWII Sampaloc poverty as fourth child of an elementary-only mother and high-school-only Bureau of Lands clerk father; hawking newspapers, cigarettes, shoeshines; born Catholic but 'unchurched and uncatechized'; father vetoing his chemical-engineering dream and insisting on law; UP scholarship he could not afford the 15-centavo bus fare to claim; failed UST entrance interview when he answered 'Jesus, Mary, Joseph' instead of the Trinity; enrolling at FEU instead, which proved 'one of the best turns of my entire life' — there he met Dean Jovito R. Salonga (whose teaching about aspiring for what money cannot buy he holds 'sacred to this day') and FEU chaplain Fr. Michael Nolan (who taught him basic Catholicism). After Ramos offered him an SC seat on June 17, 1992, seven JBC rejections over three years followed; reading Luke 11 ('ask, seek, knock') and Luke 9 ('listen to Him') on successive Sundays gave him the courage to resubmit. On September 20, 1995, he passed JBC screening for the first time; in late 2005 he was named 21st CJ. To Sister Estela's question 'Why are you who you are now?' his answer: 'Please ask the Lord and Mother Mary. I am merely their servant.'

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered on December 6, 2006 at the Supreme Court Session Hall as his retirement valedictory, CJ Panganiban structures his thanksgiving in concentric circles — first the two Presidents who appointed him (Ramos to the Court on October 10, 1995, and Arroyo as 21st Chief Justice on December 21, 2005), then his Supreme Court colleagues, his teachers from Candaba Elementary to FEU, his spiritual shepherds led by Fr. Michael Nolan and Dr. Jovito R. Salonga, and finally his wife Leni and their five children and nine grandchildren. He uses the occasion to crystallize the institutional vocabulary of what he calls 'the Panganiban Court': the 'Four Ins' the judiciary must embody (independence, integrity, industry, intelligence), the four institutional vices it must resist (influence, interference, indifference, insolence), the 'plague of ships' that corrodes impartiality (kinship, relationship, friendship, fellowship), and the 'ACID problems' that the Action Program for Judicial Reform attacks (limited Access by the poor, Corruption, Incompetence, Delay). All of these, he argues, are means toward the twin end-goals of safeguarding liberty AND nurturing prosperity under the rule of law, exemplified by the Global Forum on Liberty and Prosperity he convened October 18-20, 2006. He closes with a personal Psalm-23 echoing prayer dedicating his life to God.
